title: Chapter 36: Mia's Ghosts
content: The Mia romance quest was stuck at 28%.

The system showed her affection climbing in tiny increments—1% here, 2% there—but the pace was glacial compared to Luna's and Sarah's trajectories. Mia Santos was a fortress, and every wall she let down, she rebuilt twice as high the next day.

I tried the obvious approaches: shared meals (she ate at odd hours, alone), conversation (she answered questions with questions), physical proximity (she always positioned herself with clear sight lines and two exit routes). Nothing moved the needle.
So I stopped trying and started waiting.

In gaming terms, Mia was a late-game boss—you couldn't rush her, couldn't force the pattern, couldn't cheese the mechanics. You had to learn the fight, respect the rhythm, and wait for the opening that she'd give you when she was ready.
The opening came on Day Thirty-Eight.

I was on the roof for my nightly practice—Mia had insisted I maintain the shooting drills, and I'd actually started to enjoy the focus they required—when she appeared from the stairwell access door with a bottle of whiskey in her hand.
Not system whiskey. *Real* whiskey, aged and amber in a bottle with a label I recognized from the expensive shelf at the liquor store I'd never been able to afford.
"Where did you get that?" I asked.

"Raided the Roosevelt's private stock during the alliance meeting." She sat down next to me, not close but not far, and offered the bottle. "Colt won't miss it—he doesn't drink. Says it impairs judgment."
"That's... probably true."
"Probably." She took a swig and winced—the good kind of wince, the kind that said the whiskey was doing its job. "Doesn't mean it's not necessary sometimes."
I took the bottle and drank. The whiskey burned going down and warmed everything it touched, and for a moment the dead city below us was just a city, and the end of the world was just a bad dream.
"My brother was named Marco," Mia said, out of nowhere, into the silence between drinks.
I didn't respond. I'd learned, with Mia, that silence was a better invitation than questions.

"He was twenty-two. Two years younger than me. Army, like our father—couldn't escape the family business. He was stationed at Joint Base Lewis-McChord when the outbreak hit." Her voice was flat, controlled, the words coming out like a field report. "The base went dark on Day Two. Last communication was a distress call requesting containment support. No one came."
"I'm sorry."

"Don't be sorry. Be angry. Be angry that the government knew this was coming—my father had clearance, he told me once that there were contingency plans for a biological event, that they'd been preparing for years. And when it happened, they did nothing. They closed the doors and saved themselves and left everyone else to die."

Her hand tightened on the whiskey bottle. In the moonlight, I could see the tendons standing out in her forearm—the same hands that held a rifle with surgeon's precision, now shaking with a grief she'd been carrying alone for five weeks.
"I keep thinking," she said, "that if I'd been there—if I'd gone to visit him that weekend like I was supposed to—maybe I could have—"

"You couldn't have." I said it gently but firmly, because the thing Mia needed wasn't comfort—it was the truth. "The base was overrun. The military couldn't contain it. You'd have died with him, and you wouldn't have been able to save anyone."

"I know. I *know* that. But knowing doesn't help." She took another drink, longer this time, the whiskey disappearing in gulps that would have put a sailor to shame. "Marco was the one who taught me to shoot. He was twelve and I was fourteen, and our father was deployed, and Marco said: 'If we can shoot, we can protect each other.' So I learned. And I got good. And in the end..."
"In the end, it wasn't enough."

"It's never enough." She laughed—a broken, bitter sound. "All that training, all those hours at the range, all those competitions where I proved I was the best shot in the state. And when Marco needed me, I was forty miles away, practicing for a *tournament*."
The guilt was audible in her voice—a living thing that had been eating at her since Day One, that she'd been feeding with whiskey and isolation and the cold comfort of competence.
"Mia," I said. "Marco didn't die because you weren't there. He died because the world ended. And there's nothing—nothing—you could have done to stop it."

"Then what's the point?" She turned to face me, and her eyes were wet—not just from the whiskey—with the raw, unguarded pain of someone who'd finally stopped holding it together. "What's the point of being good at something if the people you love still die?"
I didn't have a philosophical answer. I didn't have a comforting platitude. I had one thing, and it was the only thing I'd ever had that mattered:

"The point is that you're still here. And you're using what you're good at to keep other people alive. Jessica, Rick, Maria—people who'd be dead without you. Luna, who needs overwatch because she can't see everything. Me, who'd have been eaten on Day One without you on that roof."
"That's not—"

"It's exactly enough. It's the only thing that's ever been enough. We don't get to save everyone, Mia. We get to save who we can, and we carry the rest, and we keep going because stopping is the only thing that's unforgivable."
She stared at me. The tears spilled—not dramatic, not cinematic, just a quiet overflow of something that had been under pressure for too long.
"I'm tired of carrying," she whispered.
"Then let me help." I held out my hand—not grabbing, not demanding, just offering a point of contact in the darkness. "Let us help. That's what family does."
She looked at my hand for a long time. Then she took it.
Her grip was strong—the grip of a shooter, calloused and certain—and she held on like I was a lifeline, like letting go meant drowning.
"Thank you," she said.
"For what?"
"For not trying to fix it. For just... being here."
"That's all I know how to do," I admitted. "Be here. Stay. It's worked so far."
She laughed again, but this time it was different—warmer, wetter, with the edge of genuine humor that lurked beneath her tactical exterior.
"Stay," she repeated. "You sound like your zombie girlfriend."
"She taught me the word. Best vocabulary lesson I ever had."

**QUEST UPDATE: MIA SANTOS**
**AFFECTION: 28% → 41%**
**MILESTONE: Genuine emotional vulnerability (First grief sharing)**
**NOTE: Target's walls are cracking. Continue authentic engagement. Do not exploit the opening—let her come to you.**
13% in one conversation. The biggest jump since our first meeting.
Not because I'd strategized. Not because the system had coached me. Because I'd listened, and waited, and been there when she was ready.
Love wasn't a quest objective. It was a *practice*. Something you did every day, without expectation of reward, until the walls came down on their own.

We sat on the roof until the whiskey was gone, watching the stars and talking about nothing and everything—Marco's terrible taste in music, our father's stoic inability to say "I love you," the way the world had ended and somehow left two people on a roof in the dark, holding hands.
When Mia finally went inside, she squeezed my hand once—a promise, or a beginning—and disappeared into the safe house without another word.

I stayed on the roof a while longer, looking at the city of the dead and thinking about the living—the people I'd found, the walls I'd watched them lower, the family we were building one vulnerable moment at a time.
**AFFECTION UPDATE: MIA SANTOS — 44%**
Climbing. Still climbing.
And this time, I wasn't counting the numbers.
I was counting the moments.
